title: Leucanthemum odoratum
intro: Leucanthemum odoratum, commonly known as the Scented Oxeye Daisy, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Asteraceae family, characterized by its white or pink flowers with a sweet, vanilla-like fragrance. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues, reduce fever, and protect against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Leucanthemum odoratum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpene lactones, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often used to prepare teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are commonly used to treat various health conditions, including gastrointestinal issues, respiratory problems, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Leucanthemum odoratum belongs to the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. This classification places Leucanthemum odoratum in the order Asterales, a group that includes many species of flowering plants with showy heads. The genus Leucanthemum is a group of daisy-like plants that are native to the Northern Hemisphere. Within the family Asteraceae, Leucanthemum odoratum is further classified as a member of the tribe Anthemideae. This tribe includes many species of daisy-like plants that are commonly found in a variety of habitats.
uses: Leucanthemum odoratum, commonly known as the fragrant oxeye daisy, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may help to soothe wounds and infections. properties: Leucanthemum odoratum has been reported to possess various therapeutic properties, primarily due to its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial compounds. constituents: Leucanthemum odoratum contains flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which are known for their antioxidant properties. The plant also contains sesquiterpene lactones, including leucanthemum, which may contribute to its potential therapeutic effects. Additionally, Leucanthemum odoratum contains other phytochemicals like phenolic acids and saponins, which may have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.
